+------------+--------+-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+

: Real datasets used for regionalization {#tbl-reg_datasets}

The Chicago dataset is frequently used as a showcase example in spatial data analysis, combining Airbnb listings with socioeconomic and crime statistics by community area. Nine variables were selected for the analysis, listed in @tbl-real-reg-datasets.

The Ceará Zika dataset maps Zika and microcephaly infections alongside socioeconomic variables across municipalities in the state of Ceará, Brazil. The five selected variables are listed in \@tbl-real-reg-datasets.

The South dataset contains homicide statistics and socioeconomic characteristics for counties in the southern United States. The five selected variables are listed in \@tbl-real-reg-datasets. The South dataset's region-border and local corruption were parametrized using its "STATE_FIPS" column. For local removal, each of the 17 states was selected once per sequence.

-tady by asi mely byt mapicky vsech(?) varibles

# Results

## Spatial autocorrelation

### Synthetic data

#### Moran's I

The kernel density plot (@fig-kdeplot) shows that deviations from the true Moran's I are symmetric around zero across all three ranges of true $I$, with roughly equal numbers of positive and negative deviations. Since deviations aren't systematically biased in one direction, the sign carries no additional information. From this point on, we therefore work with the absolute deviation, without loss of relevant information.

![Kernel density plot of deviations from true Moran's I. Flitered to random edge removal.](figures/moran_deviation_kde_combined.png){#fig-kdeplot fig-align="center" width="60%"}

Dataset size exerts the clearest influence on absolute deviation, with larger datasets demonstrating markably greater resilience to topology corruption (see @fig-shape_size_comapairson). For $n=400$, maximum deviation slightly exceeds 0.2, even under the most severe corruption levels examined (70-80% edges missing), whereas for $n=25$ it can reach up to 0.5 and even over 0.7 in the most extreme cases, which can critically mislead interpretation of the estimated spatial autocorrelation.

@fig-shape_size_comapairson also shows that lattice shape, which primarily distinguishes the average number of neighbors per node (cardinality), exerts a slight influence on the resulting deviations, where datasets with smaller cardinalities (square lattice) are more vulnerable, but compared to size, it does not affect it as much. Finally, across all lattice sizes, absolute deviation increases approximately linearly with the proportion of missing edges, with no distinct thresholds or stabilization points evident in the data.

![Boxplots comparing absolute deviation of $I$ based on the size and type of lattice used. Filtered to random graph corruption.](figures/size_boxplot_grouped_by_shape.png){#fig-shape_size_comapairson fig-align="center"}

@fig-moran_vulnerability shows that random spatial patterns (true Moran's $I$ close to zero) are comparatively the most vulnerable to topology corruption, with maximum absolute deviations exceeding 0.3. As $I$ increases, estimates become increasingly robust. For positive autocorrelation, extreme absolute deviations do not exceed 0.2, and for values between 0.7 and 0.9 they remain below 0.1. Strongly negatively autocorrelated patterns were generated only for the square lattice, as it is only in this configuration that such extreme negative values could be sustained. Relative to their positive counterparts, these negative patterns exhibit somewhat larger deviations

![Boxplots of absolute deviation of Global Moran's $I$ under different true $I$s. Filtered to random graph corruption and $n=100$.](figures/moran_value_vulnerability100_boxplot.png){#fig-moran_vulnerability fig-align="center" width="80%"}

Across all bins, the 75th percentile does not exceed 0.07 absolute deviation, indicating that typical estimation error remains small even under substantial topology corruption. However, the occurrence of larger deviations in the upper tail indicates that isolated cases of substantial deviation remain possible and should not be dismissed when performing spatial autocorrelation analysis.

Results were also compared based on the removal method. Across all spatial structures, global Moran's I deviated the most under local edge removal, while border removal performed comparably to random edge removal at the same level of corruption. However, even the 95th percentile only slightly exceeds 0.1 in the most corrupted local-removal case (hexagonal lattice, $n = 100$) partly because the maximum percentage of removed edges is only 20–30% under this method. Nonetheless, absolute deviations of global Moran's $I$ remain small in absolute terms, suggesting the statistic is comparatively robust to targeted corruption even though local removal consistently produces the largest deviations among the three methods tested.

![Edge removal method compaoirson.](figures/moran_deviation_methods.png){#fig-global_method_compairson fig-align="center"}

#### Statistical significance

@fig-p_scatter presents a scatterplot of true Moran's I values against their corrupted counterparts across different levels of graph corruption, demonstrated on hexagonal lattice of size 100. It confirms the symmetry of deviations observed in @fig-kdeplot and indicates that datasets exhibiting no significant spatial pattern are more vulnerable to misclassification.

@fig-p_scatter also illustrates how significance status changes relative to the critical value at $\alpha =0.05$. Specifically, it shows that whether a dataset is misclassified is not directly determined by the degree of corruption itself, but rather by whether the corrupted value crosses a critical threshold.

![Scatterplots of true vs. calculated Moran's $I$ under graph corruption, by percentage of missing edges. Filtered to $n=100$, hexagonal lattice, random edge removal.](figures/p_values4.png){#fig-p_scatter fig-align="center" width="100%"}

The false non-significance rate increases with corruption level, as illustrated in @fig-miss_sig (right), since more extensive edge removal raises the probability that a given estimate will cross the significance threshold. The magnitude of this rate also reflects dataset size: larger datasets are more stable, and therefore less vulnerable to misclassification. Interestingly, average cardinality does not correspond to greater robustness the way it does for $I$ values. Although $I$ deviates more strongly under corruption when cardinality is lower, lattices with lower average cardinality (e.g., square) show lower misclassification rates in @fig-miss_sig (right), while those with higher average cardinality (e.g., hexagonal) show higher rates.

The rate of false significance (@fig-miss_sig, left) is considerably lower than that of false non-significance. It increases up to approximately 15–20% missing edges across all lattice types and dataset sizes, after which it stabilizes and fluctuates around a rate of 8%, with no apparent influence from dataset size or cardinality.

![Missclassified statistical significance of Global Moran's $I$. Filtered to random edge removal.](figures/p_values_two_panels_by_error_type.png){#fig-miss_sig fig-align="center" width="100%"}

The occurrence of significance loss and gain as a function of $I_{true}$ is illustrated in @fig-sig_hist. Significance loss increases as $I_{true}$ moves inward from extreme values toward the critical $I$ value, at which point the observation can no longer sustain significance. Significance gain, conversely, increases as $I_{true}$ moves outward from zero toward larger absolute values, culminating at approximately the same point at which significance loss culminates - the critical $I$ value. Datasets whose $I_{true}$ lies close to the critical value are therefore the most vulnerable to significance misinterpretation.

The width of the non-significance interval is primarily determined by sample size, with larger datasets narrowing this interval substantially. Connectivity exerts a similar but visibly weaker effect, with denser lattices yielding critical values closer to zero. The critical values can be estimated analytically from the moments of $I$ established by Cliff and Ord (1981) and will be examined in further detail in the Discussion.

![Significance misclassification rate per size. Data filtered to random edge removal.](figures/moran_falsesig_falsenonsig_rate_by_size_shape_9panel.png){#fig-sig_hist fig-align="center"}

### Real data

The deviations of $I$ for real datasets shown in @fig-sac_real are consistent with the results obtained from synthetic data. The elections dataset exhibits strong robustness to corruption, with a maximum absolute deviation of 0.024 in the most corrupted case. This can be attributed both to a high positive $I_{true} = 0.62$ and to the dataset's large size ($n = 3108$). The Sacramento dataset shows greater vulnerability than the elections dataset: despite a similar true spatial autocorrelation ($I_{true} = 0.56$), this dataset is nearly eight times smaller ($n = 403$) , which is the primary driver of its increased vulnerability. However, the maximum deviation reaches only +0.11, which, given the magnitude of $I_{true}$, does not lead to a misleading conclusion.

![Moran's I deviation on real data under graph corruption plotted by independent runs (upper left, upper right, lower left) and $p$-value change of Guerry dataset (lower right). Each run is plotted separately. Data filtered to random edge removal.](figures/sac_real_overview.png){#fig-sac_real fig-align="center" width="100%"}

The Guerry dataset, with a relatively small $n = 85$ and weaker true positive spatial autocorrelation ($I_{true} = 0.35$), is considerably more fragile. In the most corrupted case, $I_{corrupt} = 0.18$, a deviation large enough to produce misleading conclusions about the underlying data. This is also the only case in which significance was lost at $\alpha = 0.05$, visualized in @fig-sac_real by the light blue line, alongside several other runs (grey, yellow, and orange lines) in which $I$ moves toward zero and approaches the significance threshold. This illustrates how a shift of $I$ toward zero can result in significance loss.

Across all real datasets, most notably for Sacramento and Guerry, @fig-sac_real shows that within the independent runs, the deviation does not grow linearly with the proportion of missing edges. Instead, it fluctuates: each run follows a distinct trajectory and in some cases additional missing edges partially cancel out prior deviation, causing the deviation to decrease even as corruption increases. This indicates that the resulting deviation is not solely a function of the amount of missing edges, but also depends on which specific edges are removed.

@fig-menhod_comparson_guerry shows that border removal sustained global Moran's I the best, with the 95th percentile not exceeding an absolute deviation of 0.025. In @fig-guerry_detail, we can see the independent runs converge onto two specific values, meaning that under maximum corruption, only two distinct graphs were generated.

![Compairson of edge removal methods on Guerry dataset. Mean is represented by full line, 95th percentile by dashed line.](figures/guerry_moran_deviation.png){#fig-menhod_comparson_guerry fig-align="center" width="66%"}

Unlike the results for synthetic data, the mean deviation of *I* for the Guerry dataset under local removal does not show greater vulnerability than under random removal. However, at the same level of corruption as random removal, it does produce higher extreme values. This is shown in @fig-guerry_detail (deviation shown after removal edge by edge), where corruption of the West region (W) is responsible for the extreme case, reaching a deviation of approximately 0.1 after deleting 17% of edges, a level of deviation that random edge removal only reaches after deleting nearly 50% of edges.

![Comparison of deviations of global Moran's I under different methods of edge removals on Guerry dataset.](figures/guerry_moran_deviation_detail.png){#fig-guerry_detail}

## Regionalization

### Synthetic data

Overall, SCHC shows greater robustness to graph corruption than SKATER. This is most visible in @fig-reg_boxplots for the hexagonal and pentagonal lattices of $n=400$, where SCHC's median ARI is noticeably higher across most of the corruption intensities. As more edges are removed, however, this gap narrows. At the highest levels of corruption the two methods converge, and in some configurations SKATER's median ARI slightly exceeds SCHC's.

SKATER's ARI distribution tends to be wider, with more extreme outliers, than SCHC's. This is most apparent for the pentagonal lattice with of $n=100$: even with minimal corruption (up to 5% edges missing), a subset of runs reach ARI values as low as approximately 0.35, and at the highest corruption level a number of runs return ARI values slightly below zero.

It is also worth noting the threshold beyond which perfect regionalization was no longer sustained, even in the best-case scenario. Both algorithms, across all lattice structures, were able to maintain a 100% correct partition up to approximately 15% missing edges; the square lattice of $n=100$ maintained this even up to 20%. Beyond this point, SKATER began producing slightly imperfect results (ARI around 0.95) between 15% and 20% missing edges, with the exception of the pentagonal lattice with $n=100$, where SCHC was the first to degrade instead. Under maximum corruption, the best-case ARI across configurations was approximately 0.7, with the exception of the square lattice with 100 units, which dropped below 0.6.

![Boxplots of computed ARI between ground truth and regionalization techniques under graph corruption. Data filtered to random edge removal.](figures/reg_boxplots.png){#fig-reg_boxplots fig-align="center"}

@fig-reg_line_plot offers a clearer comparison across the six spatial configurations for each method. The square lattice of $n=100$ performed worst overall, with one notable exception: under SKATER, it initially degraded more slowly than the other configurations, before dropping sharply below all other configurations as corruption continued. Under SKATER, both pentagonal lattices underperformed their hexagonal counterparts throughout the corruption range. Under SCHC, however, the pentagonal lattice with 400 units slightly outperformed the hexagonal lattice with 100 units up to roughly 40% missing edges. Across both methods, the rate of decline slows markedly toward the higher end of the corruption, minimum mean ARI across all six configurations and both methods falls within a narrow band of 0.33–0.39.

![Line plots comparing spatial configurations for each regionalization method.](figures/reg_lineplot_faceted.png){#fig-reg_line_plot fig-align="center"}

Compared with random edge removal, both targeted removal methods, border and local, resulted in substantially worse ARI degradation across all spatial layouts, for both algorithms. At low levels of corruption, the three removal methods perform similarly, with only small differences between them. As corruption increases, however, random removal degrades noticeably more slowly than either targeted method. For example, in the square lattice of $n=400$, both algorithms already drop to a mean ARI of around 0.4 by 15–20% missing edges under border removal, whereas random removal does not reach this level of degradation until approximately 45–50% missing edges, maintaining a mean ARI of roughly 0.85–0.9 at the same 15–20% missing level. Regionalization algorithms under local removal generally performed better or comparably to border removal at a given level of corruption.

![Method of edge removal compairson on regionalization techniques.](figures/reg_lineplot_by_method.png){#fig-reg_nevim fig-align="center" width="100%"}

### Real data

Interestingly, the regionalization performed on real data does not follow the same degraadtion pattern as the synthetic data. SCHC, which performed better across all synthetic configurations, is here comparable to or worse than SKATER. This is most visible for the Ceará Zika dataset in @fig-reg_real_all, where SCHC's mean ARI drops rapidly to approximately 0.55 at 5–10% missing edges, and falls further to around 0.3 by 25% missing edges. A similar early drop appears for both algorithms in the South dataset, most notably for SCHC, whose mean ARI already falls to approximately 5% below its starting value within the first 5% of removed edges, in contrast to the synthetic data, which remained comparatively stable at low corruption levels.

![Average ARI on real data.](figures/reg_real_all.png){#fig-reg_real_all fig-align="center"}

To showcase the specific impact of per-edge removal, regionalization was performed on graphs with edges removed one by one, up to 50 missing edges. Specific runs were selected and plotted in @fig-reg_examples. The results show that degradation is not gradual, but instead occurs in discrete jumps: removing a single additional edge can shift the resulting ARI substantially. For example, removing just two edges from the Chicago dataset caused SCHC's ARI to fall sharply to 0.55. The resulting corrupted regionalization is shown in @fig-reg_chciago.

Another example is the Ceará Zika dataset, where SCHC regionalization ARI collapsed from a perfect score (ARI = 1) to 0.29 after the removal of 16 edges, producing a markedly different regionalization outcome (see @fig-reg_zika). Interestingly, as corruption continued, the regionalization recovered almost completely: after the removal of the 20th edge, ARI rose back up to 0.98.

SKATER regionalization for South shifted noticeably after the loss of only a single edge, with ARI dropping to 0.83, demonstrating that even a minor topological inconsistency can substantially alter the outcome of the statistic (see @fig-reg_south).

![ARI example runs.](figures/reg_real_examples.png){#fig-reg_examples fig-align="center"}

![Example of Chicago dataset SCHC regionalization under graph corruption.](figures/reg_chicago_map.png){#fig-reg_chciago fig-align="center"}

![Example of Ceara Zika dataset SCHC regionalization under graph corruption.](figures/reg_ceara_zika_map.png){#fig-reg_zika}

![Example of South dataset SKATER regionalization under graph corruption.](figures/reg_south_map.png){#fig-reg_south}

On the South dataset, edge removal methods were compared (see @fig-south_method) by computing the mean ARI within 1% wide bins. To ensure comparability across methods, the maximally corrupted graph under random edge removal was standardized to 500 missing edges, which corresponds to roughly 13 % missing edges . Borders removal yielded the poorest performance across both clustering algorithms, consistent with the results observed on the synthetic datasets. This pattern does not hold for local removal, however, which performs comparably to regionalization accuracy under random edge removal. The pronounced peak observed at the 9–10% interval is likely attributable to edge deletions concentrated within a specific region, where a greater number of edges were eligible for removal; this bin should therefore be interpreted with caution given its correspondingly small sample size.

![Edge removal methods compared on South dataset.](figures/south_extra_lineplot_by_method.png){#fig-south_method fig-align="center" width="75%"}

# Discussion

-jak je mozny ze je deviation symetricka? pri densyfying to jde k nule, nemelo by to jit vic do extremu?

\- critical values a ten mechanismus, see below

The critical $I$ values that set the significance threshold can be estimated analytically using the moments derived by Cliff and Ord (1981), namely expected value $E[I]$ and variance $Var(I)$. Under the randomization assumption, the expectation and variance. Critically, $Var(I)$ depends on the structure of $W$, so the threshold is fully determined by $W$'s connectivity pattern together with one data-dependent scalar, curtosis $b_2$. The critical values follow directly by inverting the test statistic:

$$I_{crit} = E[I] \pm z_\alpha\sqrt{\text{Var}(I)}$$

As edges are removed, $I'_{crit}$ is pushed further from zero - sparser weight matrices produce a wider, harder-to-clear significance band <!--(Bivand, Müller, and Reder 2009; Cliff and Ord 1981)-->. This means graph corruption has two distinct effects: it perturbs the calculated $I'$ away from its true value, and it independently shifts the critical threshold itself.

Significance loss occurs when $|I| > |I_{crit}|$ and $|I'| < |I'_{crit}|$. As corruption increases, $|I'_{crit}|$ grows, causing a larger share of computed values $I'$ to fall within the widened non-significant interval. Notably, even where $I'$ moves away from zero, $I'_{crit}$ may still exceed it, since the threshold shifts independently of the realized value of $I'$.

This mechanism operates in the opposite direction for significance gain, which occurs when $|I| < |I_{crit}|$ and $|I'| > |I'_{crit}|$. If $I'$ moves toward zero under corruption, the observation necessarily remains non-significant. If $I'$ moves away from zero, it may in principle exceed the threshold; however, this outcome is comparatively rare, as $I'_{crit}$ is simultaneously shifting in the same direction. This asymmetry explains why significance gain occurs substantially less frequently than significance loss.

-na local removal citlivejsi asi protoze matrix heterogenenity a neco s tim S1, S2?

-porvnani real a synthtic dat, zduraznit, ze pravidelny lattices maji sva omezeni (nacaty v backgorundu)

-brutalni citlivost regionalizace, especially u real dat, je to hard to predict, a jelikoz jsou to oba greedy algoritmy, tak jakmile se to jednou posere, neni cesty zpet

-logicky asi se regionalizace posere, kdyz odebereme edge, ktera by v nejaky kroku mela nejvetsi/nejmensi distance mezi clustery a v pripade skateru mst

-efekt na regionalizaci je obyvlast understudied, jelikoz contiguty weights jsou jedina spravna volba

-morans i se meni relatively smoothly, ale regionalizace se odhaduje dost blbe.. kazdej odebranej edge s moranem neco udela, ale u regionalizace muze a nemusi,
